data.title: QNB Corp. Reports Earnings for Fourth Quarter 2025
data.description:
QNB Corp., the parent company of QNB Bank, has released its financial results for the fourth quarter of 2025. The company reported a net income of $3.98 million, or $1.06 per diluted share, marking a significant increase from the $3.05 million, or $0.83 per diluted share, reported in the same period of 2024. For the full year ending December 31, 2025, QNB Corp. reported a net income of $14.09 million, or $3.78 per diluted share, compared to $11.45 million, or $3.12 per diluted share, in 2024.

Financial Highlights

QNB Corp. demonstrated strong financial performance in the fourth quarter of 2025, with notable improvements in several key metrics:

  • Net interest income for the quarter was $14.04 million, up from $10.98 million in the same period of 2024.
  • Net interest margin increased to 2.95% from 2.38% in the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Total assets as of December 31, 2025, were $1.91 billion, compared to $1.87 billion at the end of 2024.
  • Loans receivable grew by 3.8% to $1.26 billion.
  • Total deposits increased by 0.9% to $1.64 billion.

Business and Operational Highlights

The bank's operating performance improved significantly, driven by an increase in net interest income and non-interest income. The interest margin improvement led to a $3.11 million increase in net interest income, while non-interest income rose by $227,000. However, these gains were partially offset by a $1.05 million increase in non-interest expenses.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

On September 23, 2025, QNB Corp. announced a definitive agreement to acquire The Victory Bancorp, Inc. in an all-stock transaction. This merger is expected to create a bank holding company with nearly $2.4 billion in assets. The transaction is anticipated to close in the second quarter of 2026, pending regulatory and shareholder approvals.

Management's Perspective

Dave Freeman, President and CEO of QNB Corp., commented, "We are proud to report another quarter of improved operating performance, highlighted by record net interest income, strong margin expansion, and continued loan growth. These results demonstrate the resilience of our customers and the dedication of our team. The upcoming merger with Victory Bancorp represents a pivotal step forward, enabling us to deepen our community impact and create greater opportunities for our shareholders and customers alike."

Future Outlook

The company expects the merger with Victory Bancorp to be completed in the second quarter of 2026, subject to regulatory and shareholder approvals. This strategic move is anticipated to enhance the company's market position and financial performance in the coming years.

Original SEC Filing: QNB CORP. [ QNBC ] - 8-K - Jan. 27, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Community Bancorp. Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Community Bancorp., the parent company of Community National Bank, has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and full year ending December 31, 2025. The company reported a significant increase in earnings, driven by strong loan growth and higher yields.

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Community Bancorp. reported consolidated earnings of $4.6 million or $0.83 per share, marking a 13.10% increase from the $4.1 million or $0.73 per share reported for the same period in 2024. Full year earnings for 2025 were $17 million or $3.01 per share, compared to $12.8 million or $2.28 per share for the prior year, reflecting a 32.93% increase.

Key financial metrics include a return on average assets of 1.41% for the year and 1.49% for the fourth quarter, and a net interest margin of 3.68% for the year and 3.81% for the fourth quarter. The efficiency ratio improved slightly to 57.0% for the year and 56.5% for the quarter.

Business and Operational Highlights

Total assets for the company increased by $38.6 million to $1.29 billion, a 3.09% year-over-year growth. The gross loan portfolio grew by $37 million, or 4.02%, and deposit balances increased by $69 million, or 6.89%, compared to the same period in 2024. The company's securities portfolio decreased by 9.50% to $144 million, as cash flow from maturing securities was used to fund loan growth.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Equity capital increased to $113.7 million, with a book value per share of $20.36 as of December 31, 2025. The company completed the optional redemption of all fifteen outstanding shares of its Series A Fixed-to-Floating Rate Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The company declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.25 per share payable on February 1, 2026, to shareholders of record as of January 15, 2026.

Management's Perspective

President and CEO Christopher Caldwell commented on the results, highlighting the company's strong performance and disciplined approach to managing the loan and deposit sides of the balance sheet. He emphasized the company's focus on credit quality, efficient use of capital, and maximizing shareholder return. Caldwell also expressed gratitude to the dedicated team and loyal stakeholders for their support and contributions to the company's growth and progress.

Future Outlook

The company remains optimistic about its future, with a focus on maintaining strong financial performance and customer service. As Community National Bank celebrates its 175th anniversary, the leadership team is confident in their ability to navigate the economic environment and continue delivering solid financial results.

Original SEC Filing: COMMUNITY BANCORP /VT [ CMTV ] - 8-K - Jan. 27, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: US judiciary may not be able to fully maintain operations past Feb. 4 in government shutdown
data.description:
By Nate Raymond

The federal courts may not be able to fully maintain paid operations beyond February 4 if Congress fails to prevent a partial government shutdown by passing funding legislation for the judicial branch and federal agencies, including the U.S. Department of Homeland Security, by midnight on Friday, a top judge has warned.

Judge Robert Conrad, the director of the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, shared that assessment in an internal memo on Monday afternoon as Senate Democrats vowed to oppose a funding bill for DHS after immigration officers on Saturday fatally shot a U.S. citizen, Alex Pretti, in Minnesota.

The legislation funding the department, which oversees U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, is part of a six-bill spending package covering various areas of the federal government, including defense, health, transportation, education, and housing for the fiscal year ending September 30.

Those bills had already cleared the Republican-led U.S. House of Representatives and include a measure that would provide the judiciary $9.2 billion, up 6.4% from a year earlier. The Homeland Security bill also provides an extra $30 million for Supreme Court security.

The funding package had appeared to be on track for passage in the Senate, which had already approved bills to fund other parts of the government, until Saturday, when immigration officers shot and killed Pretti.

His death prompted Democrats in the Republican-led Senate to vow not to support further DHS funding without reforms to safeguard Americans, raising the chances of parts of the government shuttering just months after the previous record shutdown ended in November.

"The combined bill no longer appears to have sufficient votes to pass the Senate, and a lapse of some duration is a distinct possibility while a strategy to address DHS funding is developed," Conrad wrote in Monday's memo to judges nationwide.

If approved funding lapses, the judiciary would not immediately shut down and employees should report to work as normal on February 2, Conrad wrote. But he said it can only rely on fees and other balances to sustain paid operations through February 4.

That would be a very short period compared to the most recent shutdown, when the judiciary was able to sustain paid operations for over two weeks after the lapse in appropriations began on October 1 before curtailing operations and furloughing employees.

Read more:

Shutdown looms as ICE shootings spawn partisan fight over DHS funding in US Congress

US court security, public defenders to receive funding boost in spending bill

US courts set to run out of money, begin furloughs as shutdown lingers

data.title: Soy, corn basis steady-firm amid slow grain movement
data.description:
Spot basis bids for soybeans and corn were steady to firmer in the U.S. Midwest on Tuesday, reflecting demand from processors and other grain handlers, while wintry weather slowed grain movement and languishing Chicago Board of Trade futures prices curbed farmer sales, brokers said.

  • "Very cold, blustery conditions prevail throughout the Corn Belt, with this morning's minimum temperatures falling below zero degrees Fahrenheit (minus 18 degrees Celsius) from the eastern Dakotas into the upper Great Lakes region," the U.S. Department of Agriculture said in a daily weather note.

  • The spot basis for soybeans improved by 7 cents a bushel at Decatur, Illinois, a major processing site, and by 3 cents at an elevator in Cincinnati, Ohio.

  • The corn basis rose by 2 cents at Cincinnati as well. Basis bids held steady elsewhere, spot checks showed.

  • CBOT March (CH26) corn futures headed lower for a second session on Tuesday and remained 10 to 20 cents a bushel below the trading range from early January, before the U.S. Department of Agriculture reported larger-than-expected U.S. corn stockpiles.

  • CBOT March (SH26) soybean futures were trying to rally from multi-month lows set in mid-January, but the contract has not traded above $11 a bushel since mid-December.

  • As of 11:45 a.m. CST (1745 GMT), CBOT March corn (CH26) was down 1 cent at $4.27-1/4 a bushel while March soybeans (SH26) were up 6 cents at $10.67-3/4 a bushel.

data.title: Gasoline refining margins dip lower
data.description:
Northwest European gasoline refinery profit margins fell by $1.38 a barrel to $6.94 on Tuesday, Reuters calculations showed.

  • About 22,000 metric tons of Eurobob E5 gasoline traded on barges in the Argus window, which TotalEnergies and Trafigura sold to BP, Varo and Shell.

  • No Eurobob E10 barges traded in the Argus window on Tuesday.

  • In the Platts window, BP sold Varo a January 30 - February 3 barge of Eurobob E5 at $629.

  • Meanwhile, EU-27 and UK gasoline and blending component exports to other regions have averaged 839,000 barrels per day so far in January, against 811,000 bpd over the whole of December.

data.title: PREVIEW: Texas Instruments seen reporting profit dip as costs rise
data.description:
** Shares of Texas Instruments are up 0.44% to $197.44 ahead of the chipmaker's quarterly earnings result due after markets close on Tuesday

** Analysts expect TXN to report ~11% growth in revenue to $4.44 bln but a slight 0.5% drop in adj EPS to $1.29 as costs rise

** TXN's rev and adj EPS have met or exceeded expectations seven times over the last 8 quarters

** In December, Goldman Sachs analysts downgraded TXN to "sell", citing lack of cyclical margin expansion relative to peers

** In the prior quarter, TXN cast a dour revenue outlook as the chipmaker seeks to cut its exposure to Trump administration's tariff policies

** TXN is up 13.9% YTD vs NASDAQ Composite Index's 2.6% increase.

data.title: Louis Vuitton Owner LVMH Closes Year-End Quarter With Weak Sales Growth — 3rd Update
data.description:
By Mauro Orru

LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton wrapped up last year's final quarter with sluggish sales growth, a sign that shoppers weren't splurging on its collections of high-end garments and handbags in the runup to the holiday season.

The French luxury-goods giant posted fourth-quarter sales of 22.72 billion euros ($27 billion), up 1% organically. Analysts had forecast 22.59 billion euros in sales and an organic decline of 0.3%, according to Visible Alpha.

LVMH's fashion and leather goods division, which houses brands like Louis Vuitton and Dior, contributed 10.16 billion euros in sales, down 3% organically. Sales at perfumes and cosmetics declined 1%, while the wines and spirits division reported a 9% contraction in sales. Selective retailing, the unit behind Sephora, fared better, with a 7% increase in sales, while watches and jewelry logged 8% growth.

For LVMH and the wider luxury-goods sector, the final quarter represents a key test of customers' willingness to indulge on nonessential items in the runup to Black Friday, Thanksgiving and Christmas.

Earlier this month, British trench-coat maker Burberry Group, Italian luxury-fashion house Brunello Cucinelli and Cartier owner Compagnie Financiere Richemont all reported higher sales for the quarter, raising the bar for industry bellwether LVMH.

Weak sales growth shows that LVMH's collections aren't appealing to clients and that the group is still contending with a slowdown in spending for luxury goods that has plagued the industry for years.

Demand weakened considerably after a postpandemic boom, especially among less affluent shoppers. The downturn has been particularly acute in China--a key market for LVMH and its rivals--as shoppers there have been holding back spending.

Last year brought a dose of uncertainty for LVMH and the sector as it took several months for the European Union to reach a trade deal with the U.S. after President Trump announced his Liberation Day tariffs.

Luxury goods are particularly sensitive to trans-Atlantic trade frictions and the specter of tariffs has never fully disappeared despite that trade deal. Last week, LVMH and other luxury stocks slumped after Trump threatened 10% levies on various European countries he said were opposed to a U.S. takeover of Greenland. He subsequently called off those tariffs.

LVMH closed 2025 with 80.81 billion euros in annual sales, down 1% organically. Analysts had forecast 80.65 billion euros in 2025 sales with a 1.8% organic decline, according to Visible Alpha.

The group said revenue declined in Europe in the second half of the year, while the U.S. benefited from solid demand. Sales in Japan were down from 2024, but the company said it had seen a noticeable improvement in trends in the rest of Asia, citing a return to growth in the second half of the year.

In an earnings call, executives expressed confidence for 2026 despite an uncertain geopolitical and macroeconomic environment, saying the positive trends they started to see in the second half were still there.

Net profit slid 13% on year to 10.88 billion euros, while profit from recurring operations fell 9% to nearly 17.76 billion euros. Analysts had forecast net profit of 10.55 billion euros and profit from recurring operations of 17.15 billion euros, according to Visible Alpha.

The group said it would propose a dividend of 13 euros a share at its shareholders' meeting on April 23, the same as the previous year.

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: Kimberly-Clark Benefits from Innovation Across Product Tiers — Market Talk
data.description: Kimberly-Clark has been able to sell more of its products, even as consumers remain under pressure, because of its investments in innovation at every product tier, CEO Mike Hsu says. "We're growing because what we're doing is strengthening our offerings at every rung," Hsu says during a call with analysts. "We're continuing to bring great innovation at the top end, and then we are rushing that innovation through into our value tiers." Hsu says consumers are noticing that, and are responding well to the company's efforts to offer competitive costs. Organic sales rose 2.1% in the company's latest quarter, boosted by volume growth and partially offset by some investments to improve value propositions. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Analysts Forecast Increase In U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

U.S. crude oil inventories likely rose for a third consecutive week amid lower refinery use, while product stocks are seen as mixed with increases in gasoline and a decline in diesel, according to a survey by The Wall Street Journal.

Commercial crude oil stocks are expected to have risen by 1 million barrels to 427 million barrels in the week ended Jan. 23, according to the average estimate of nine analysts and traders. Seven expect an increase and two predict a decline. Expectations range from a rise of 3.1 million barrels to a withdrawal of 3 million barrels.

Gasoline inventories are expected to be up by 1 million barrels at 258 million barrels in an 11th consecutive weekly build, with estimates ranging from an increase of 3.9 million barrels to a decline of 3 million barrels.

Stocks of distillate fuel, mostly diesel, are seen falling by 1.4 million barrels to 131.2 million barrels, with forecasts ranging from a 4.7 million barrel drop to a 2.5 million barrel rise.

Refinery capacity use likely fell by 0.6 of a percentage point to 92.7%, according to the survey. Forecasts range from a 0.6 percentage point rise to a 1.2 percentage point decline. Two analysts didn't forecast refinery runs.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release the inventory data on Wednesday at 10:30 a.m. EST.

data.title: Honduras' Asfura takes office after razor-thin election win
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nasry Asfura assumes presidency in Honduras after a highly contentious election
  • Asfura pledged to restore diplomatic relations with Taiwan
  • Washington aims to begin trade negotiations "as soon as possible" with Honduras

By Laura Garcia

Conservative politician and businessman Nasry Asfura was sworn in as president of Honduras on Tuesday after a closely fought election marred by allegations of fraud and political tension fueled by U.S. interference.

Asfura, 67, will serve a four-year term until January 2030. He has pledged to fight poverty, corruption and crime, and to revive the economy in one of the poorest countries in the Western Hemisphere.

"We have to get to work; work with humility and with the total commitment required to bring real solutions to every corner of our beloved Honduras," Asfura said in a ceremony at the National Congress.

"The clock has started ticking - there is no time to waste. We have to solve people's problems to serve," he added.

Asfura has also pledged to restore diplomatic relations with Taiwan, which were severed by predecessor Xiomara Castro in 2023. Such a move would be a major diplomatic setback for China in Central America.

A former mayor of Tegucigalpa from 2014 to 2022, Asfura was inaugurated after months of electoral chaos in Honduras, fueled by a last-minute endorsement from U.S. President Donald Trump, a deeply troubled vote-counting process and a razor-thin victory of 26,000 votes over centrist candidate Salvador Nasralla.

Washington said in recent days it aimed to begin negotiations "as soon as possible" on a bilateral trade agreement with Honduras.

Still, Asfura enters office facing a delicate balancing act. While his party holds a simple majority, he will need the support of rival parties - including those that alleged voter fraud - in order to ratify international treaties or amend the constitution.

Asfura campaigned on a message of "work and more work," promising to attract investment, create jobs and impose austerity in government spending.

"In Honduras, everything is urgent," said political analyst Luis Leon in Tegucigalpa. "But two issues will determine whether people feel the government is responding: public health and investment to generate jobs."

Last year, government health workers went on strike for nearly a month to protest overdue pay as well as shortages of medicines and supplies.

A recent report by the World Bank, the Pan American Health Organization and journal The Lancet said Honduras' public health system has deep structural weaknesses that leave it highly vulnerable to health crises.

Outgoing leftist Castro leaves office after expanding public investment and social spending. Her administration oversaw moderate economic growth and declines in poverty and inequality, though both remain high.

The homicide rate fell to its lowest level in recent history, but human-rights groups criticized Castro for maintaining a prolonged state of emergency in parts of the country to combat gang violence and for expanding the role of the military.

data.title: UnitedHealth Triggers an Uncomfortable Reality Check
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group took a sharp hit Tuesday, sliding alongside its peers after quarterly results and a soft revenue outlook added to the growing sense that managed care is in a rough stretch.

The stock fell about 12% in premarket trading, while Humana dropped roughly 14%, as investors reacted to another reminder that Medicare pricing pressure is squeezing the entire industry. UnitedHealth delivered adjusted EPS of $2.11 for Q4, right in line with expectations, but revenue of $113.2 billion missed estimates by about $520 million despite growing nearly 12% year over year. That miss was enough to spook a market already on edge.

Looking ahead, guidance did little to calm nerves. UnitedHealth expects more than $439 billion in revenue for 2026, well below the roughly $456 billion Wall Street had penciled in, even as adjusted EPS guidance of over $17.75 came in roughly as expected. Costs remain a problem, with the medical care ratio jumping to 92.4% from 89.1% a year earlier, and a $1.6 billion post-tax restructuring charge weighing on the quarter.

data.description:
Stockholders approved the merger agreement for PotlatchDeltic to merge with a Rayonier subsidiary, while rejecting the proposed executive compensation related to the merger. The adjournment proposal was not needed, and final results will be filed with the SEC.

data.title: US NUCLEAR CORP. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
US NUCLEAR CORP., a leading provider of radiation and tritium monitoring solutions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational activities, providing insights into its revenue streams, geographical market performance, and future business outlook.

Financial Highlights

Sales: $1.52 million, reflecting a decrease of 12.8% compared to the same period in 2024 due to a decrease in sales from both Optron and Overhoff subsidiaries.

Gross Profit: $0.90 million, representing a decrease of 17.3% from the same period in 2024, primarily due to the mix of products sold and fluctuations in material costs.

Loss from operations: $(0.94) million, an increase in loss by 62.7% compared to the same period in 2024, driven by increased selling, general, and administrative expenses.

Net income/(loss): $(0.62) million, showing an improvement of 14.8% from the same period in 2024, largely due to settlement cash received from the termination of MIFTI and MIFTEC agreements.

Net income/(loss) per share attributable to common stockholders—basic: $(0.01), reflecting the impact of preferred stock dividends and net loss.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company operates through two main segments: Optron and Overhoff. Optron focuses on radiation monitors, while Overhoff specializes in tritium monitors. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, Overhoff contributed significantly to the company's revenue, accounting for a substantial portion of sales.

Geographical Performance: During the nine months ended September 30, 2025, North America was the primary market, contributing 92.78% of total sales. Asia, including Japan, accounted for 3.17%, and other regions contributed 4.05%. The company anticipates growth in international markets, particularly in South Korea and China, as nuclear technology and clean energy development expand.

Sales Units: The company highlighted a reliance on Overhoff's tritium monitors, which are crucial for CANDU reactors and next-generation nuclear reactors like Molten Salt Reactors and Liquid-Fluoride Thorium Reactors.

Future Outlook: The company plans to seek approximately $5,000,000 in additional capital to fund its business plans over the next twelve months. This capital is intended to support growth initiatives and address potential market challenges, such as competition and fluctuations in natural gas prices.

Operational Challenges: The company faces challenges due to its heavy reliance on a few key customers, with two customers accounting for 64% of total revenues during the nine months ended September 30, 2025. This concentration risk highlights the need for diversification in the customer base.

Management Insights: Management is focused on diversifying the product line to enhance customer experience and competitiveness. The company is also exploring opportunities to expand its international presence, particularly in regions with growing demand for nuclear technology.

SEC Filing: US NUCLEAR CORP. [ UCLE ] - 10-Q - Jan. 27, 2026
